title: Rebellion, empty worship, and cleansing
summary_text: Isaiah opens by indicting Judah as a rebellious nation whose sacrifices mean nothing without justice and mercy. The Lord calls them to wash, make themselves clean, and defend the oppressed instead of multiplying religious noise. He offers cleansing and restoration, but warns that stubborn rebels will be consumed by judgment.
key_points: God rejects offerings that are detached from obedience.
He commands justice for the vulnerable and repentance from evil.
Scarlet sins can be made white if the people are willing to hear.
Zion's future depends on whether she turns back to the Lord.
evidence_phrases: though your sins be as scarlet

title: The mountain of the Lord and the day of pride
summary_text: The chapter begins with a vision of the Lord's house exalted above all mountains, drawing the nations to learn his ways and beat their weapons into tools of peace. That hope is followed by a sweeping exposure of human pride, idolatry, and self-exaltation. The day of the Lord will humble every lofty thing so that only God remains high.
key_points: The nations will come to the house of the God of Jacob.
Weapons of war will be turned into instruments of peace.
Human pride and idols cannot stand before the Lord's glory.
The proud will flee when the Lord rises to shake the earth.
evidence_phrases: come, and let us go up

title: Judgment on leaders and the daughters of Zion
summary_text: Judah's support structures collapse as the Lord removes bread, leadership, and public stability from Jerusalem. The nation's rulers devour the vineyard instead of protecting it, while the women of Zion are judged for proud display and vanity. The chapter shows that social ruin follows moral ruin when a people persist in rebellion.
key_points: The Lord removes the supports that keep a society standing.
Rulers consume the vineyard and crush the poor.
Proud daughters are judged for their outward show.
What looks strong becomes a stench when God's order is removed.
evidence_phrases: my people, children are their oppressors

title: The branch of the Lord and the cleansed remnant
summary_text: After judgment, the chapter shows a remnant in Zion who are written for life and made holy by the Lord's cleansing work. The humiliation of the city's women is replaced by the beauty and glory of God's presence over Mount Zion. The vision moves from shame to shelter, promising that the branch of the Lord will become the joy of survivors.
key_points: Those left in Jerusalem are called holy after cleansing.
The branch of the Lord becomes beautiful and glorious.
A cloud and fire protect the city as in the wilderness.
God's presence replaces the shame of former judgment.
evidence_phrases: a cloud and smoke by day

title: The vineyard song and six woes
summary_text: Isaiah sings of a carefully planted vineyard that produced wild grapes instead of justice and righteousness. The song becomes a courtroom charge against greed, drunkenness, mocking unbelief, and those who call evil good. The final image is of a people who have rejected the Lord's instruction and now face the darkness they chose.
key_points: God expected justice, but found oppression and bloodshed.
Self-indulgence and social corruption invite woe.
People who twist moral boundaries will be judged for it.
Rejecting God's word leaves a nation under darkness.

title: The throne vision and Isaiah's commissioning
summary_text: Isaiah sees the Lord high and lifted up, with seraphim crying that the whole earth is full of his glory. Overwhelmed by holiness, he confesses his uncleanness until a coal from the altar purges his lips. Then he volunteers for the mission and is sent to speak to a people who will hear without understanding until only a holy seed remains.
key_points: God's holiness exposes human uncleanness.
A coal from the altar symbolically cleanses the prophet.
Isaiah accepts a difficult commission to a resistant people.
Judgment and mercy will leave a surviving remnant.

title: Ahaz, the sign of Immanuel, and trust denied
summary_text: When Judah trembles before Syria and Ephraim, Isaiah tells Ahaz not to fear and offers a sign from the Lord. Ahaz refuses to ask, but the Lord gives Immanuel as the sign that God is with his people and that the threatening kingdoms will not endure. Yet the chapter also warns that Assyria will come as a sharp instrument of judgment because unbelief seeks rescue from the wrong place.
key_points: The Lord tells Ahaz not to fear the two invading kings.
Immanuel is given as a sign that God is with Judah.
A child will mark the span of coming judgment and deliverance.
Trusting foreign alliances cannot replace trust in the Lord.

title: The coming invasion and the call to fear God
summary_text: Isaiah's sign-child and the swift spoil of Assyria warn that judgment is approaching quickly. The people are told not to call conspiracy what God has ordained, and not to fear the threats of men. Instead they must bind up the testimony, look to the law, and wait for the Lord rather than wandering in darkness.
key_points: The sign-child marks the speed of coming plunder.
Believers should fear God rather than popular panic.
The law and testimony are the proper measure of truth.
Those who reject God's light will walk in distress.
evidence_phrases: to the law and to the testimony

title: Light in Galilee and the child on David's throne
summary_text: After gloom in the north, the chapter promises great light in Galilee and names the coming ruler as a child born and a son given. His government, peace, and justice will rest on David's throne forever, while proud violence and corrupt leadership are consumed by the Lord's anger. The prophetic hope is that God's zeal will finally establish what human power cannot sustain.
key_points: Light dawns for the people walking in darkness.
A child will bear government, peace, and divine titles.
Violence and arrogance will be cut down like fuel for fire.
The Lord's zeal will accomplish the promise.
evidence_phrases: the people who walked in darkness

title: Assyria as the rod, then judged for arrogance
summary_text: The chapter condemns rulers who legislate injustice and prey on the poor, then explains that Assyria is being used as the Lord's rod against a wayward nation. But Assyria's own pride will not go unpunished, because the instrument cannot boast against the hand that wields it. A remnant will return to the Mighty God, and the forest of enemies will fall before him.
key_points: Bad laws and oppression bring woe on leaders.
Assyria is used to discipline but not to dominate forever.
Human arrogance is judged even when it serves God's purpose.
Only a remnant returns to the Lord in faith.
evidence_phrases: woe to those who decree iniquitous decrees
boast against him who chops with it

title: The shoot from Jesse and the peaceable kingdom
summary_text: A righteous ruler rises from Jesse's stump, filled with the Spirit and marked by perfect justice. His reign brings a transformed creation where the wolf lives with the lamb and the earth is full of the knowledge of the Lord. The chapter ends with the scattered people gathered again in unity and thanksgiving.
key_points: The Spirit rests on the coming ruler from Jesse.
He judges with righteousness and strikes the wicked.
Creation itself is pictured as reconciled and peaceful.
God gathers his people from the nations and the islands.
evidence_phrases: a shoot out of the stock of Jesse
the wolf will live with the lamb

title: The song of salvation and praise
summary_text: After the promise of restoration, the chapter becomes a brief hymn of thanksgiving for God's deliverance. The people confess that he has turned away anger, become their salvation, and made them confident to draw water from the wells of joy. Salvation is no longer a theory but a song to be sung among the nations.
key_points: God's anger is turned away from his people.
The Lord himself becomes strength, song, and salvation.
Joy is pictured as drawing water from a living spring.
The praise of Israel is to be heard among all the earth.

title: The burden of Babylon
summary_text: Isaiah announces the day of the Lord against Babylon, with armies gathered and cosmic signs shaking creation. The proud city will be made a desolation, and the Medes will be the instrument of its fall. The oracle shows that empires that exalt themselves will be brought low in a single divine act.
key_points: Babylon is summoned for destruction.
The day of the Lord shakes heaven and earth.
The Medes are raised up as an instrument of judgment.
Prideful nations are made desolate before God.

title: Taunt over Babylon's king and the fallen star
summary_text: The fall of Babylon becomes a taunt song celebrating the end of oppression and the relief of the nations. The king's pride is exposed as he is brought down to Sheol, and the image of the fallen morning star captures the shame of self-exaltation. The chapter closes with the Lord's certainty that no plan against him can stand.
key_points: The oppressed finally sing because the tyrant has fallen.
The proud king is cast down from his imagined height.
Arrogance that tries to ascend to heaven ends in the grave.
God's counsel remains firm while empires pass away.
evidence_phrases: you are brought down to Sheol

title: Moab's lament and mourning
summary_text: This oracle turns toward Moab and pictures a land filled with weeping, shaved heads, and public lament. The cities of the region are ruined and the fugitives go out with only sorrow for their portion. The tone is deeply mournful, showing how judgment can leave even a proud nation stripped of refuge.
key_points: Moab's cities are struck and its people weep openly.
Pride is not enough to shield a nation from grief.
Even the roads are filled with refugees and cries of distress.
Judgment leaves the once-secure land exposed and empty.

title: Moab's plea for refuge and a merciful throne
summary_text: Moab is urged to send tribute and seek shelter in Zion, but pride and instability make its future uncertain. The prophet laments Moab's fate and pleads for mercy even as judgment moves forward. The chapter points to a throne established in steadfast love, contrasting human arrogance with God's mercy.
key_points: Moab is told to seek shelter under Zion's protection.
Pride keeps Moab from lasting security.
The prophet pleads for compassion even under judgment.
A throne is established in steadfast love and truth.

title: Damascus falls and the remnant turns from idols
summary_text: Damascus and Ephraim are announced as soon-to-be ruined, and their glory will fade like a harvested field. Yet even in this judgment a remnant remains, turning away from altars and idols to the Holy One of Israel. The chapter balances loss with a small but real return to faith.
key_points: The glory of Damascus and Ephraim will be stripped away.
The fortified city becomes like a forgotten heap.
A remnant will look to its Maker instead of idols.
Judgment can become a doorway to renewed trust.

title: The land beyond the rivers and the Lord's harvest
summary_text: The chapter addresses a distant land marked by swift messengers and calls attention to God's patient watching over the nations. At the right time, before harvest, the Lord quietly cuts down the proud and receives tribute from a far country. The vision turns foreign attention toward Zion without needing human diplomacy.
key_points: God watches nations from his own dwelling place.
Judgment comes at the moment he chooses, not ours.
A distant people will eventually bring tribute to Zion.
The Lord can gather attention from beyond the rivers of Ethiopia.
evidence_phrases: all you inhabitants of the world

title: Egypt judged, healed, and joined to worship
summary_text: Egypt is thrown into confusion by civil strife, economic collapse, and the failure of its idols and wisdom. Yet the Lord also pictures a surprising future in which Egypt, Assyria, and Israel are bound together in blessing. Judgment therefore serves a larger purpose: to expose false trust and then heal the nations under the true King.
key_points: Egypt's social order collapses under God's hand.
Its idols and wise men cannot explain the crisis.
The Lord will strike and then heal Egypt.
Egypt, Assyria, and Israel will one day worship together.
evidence_phrases: a highway from Egypt to Assyria

title: Isaiah's sign against Egypt and Ethiopia
summary_text: Isaiah walks naked and barefoot for three years as a sign that Egypt and Ethiopia will go into captivity. The shocking act is meant to strip away false confidence in political allies and show how shameful it is to trust in human power. The lesson is simple but severe: nations are not safe because they look strong.
key_points: Isaiah's public action is itself a prophecy.
Egypt and Ethiopia will be led away captive.
Assyria will embarrass those who trusted in them.
Visible strength is no substitute for God's protection.

title: Watchman oracles over Babylon, Edom, and Arabia
summary_text: A watchman sees Babylon fall, hears the night drag on in tension, and then announces that morning is coming only after judgment. The chapter also gives brief oracles against Edom and Arabia, showing that no region is beyond the Lord's notice. The repeated image of the watchman captures the strain of waiting for God's word to be fulfilled.
key_points: The watchman sees disaster coming and cannot hasten it.
The burden over Babylon announces its collapse.
Edom's answer is uncertain and unsettling.
Arabia's refuge will also be shortened by judgment.

title: The valley of vision and the key of David
summary_text: Jerusalem panics while God calls the city to mourning instead of revelry, because disaster has reached the gate. The prophet rebukes leaders who rely on their own planning, including Shebna, and announces Eliakim as a more faithful steward. The key of David becomes a picture of delegated authority under God's sovereign hand.
key_points: The city is called to weeping, not celebration.
Self-protective leadership cannot save Jerusalem.
Shebna's pride will be removed from office.
Eliakim receives authority under the Lord's appointment.
evidence_phrases: a nail in a sure place

title: The fall and future use of Tyre
summary_text: Tyre's trade and fame are shattered so that merchants and rulers alike must grieve over lost wealth. The sea power that seemed permanent is shown to be temporary under the Lord's decree. Yet the chapter also hints at a later restoration, when Tyre's gain will be devoted to the Lord's purpose rather than pride.
key_points: Tyre's wealth and shipping are brought low.
All who profited from her grieve her ruin.
No economic empire is permanent before God.
The Lord can later redirect wealth to holy use.

title: The earth laid waste and the remnant's praise
summary_text: The judgment widens from city to world as the earth is made waste because its inhabitants have broken God's covenant. Joy disappears from the land, and even the drunkards and the proud are forced into silence. Yet a remnant still lifts praise to the Lord from the ends of the earth, anticipating final vindication.
key_points: The whole earth suffers under covenant-breaking sin.
Festivity and wine-drinking cannot stop divine judgment.
Pride is brought low, even among the rulers of the earth.
A faithful remnant still glorifies the Lord.
evidence_phrases: they shall lift up their voice

title: The feast on Mount Zion and swallowed death
summary_text: Isaiah praises God for turning strong cities into ruins and for becoming a shelter to the poor and needy. On Mount Zion the Lord will prepare a feast, remove the veil of death, and wipe tears from every face. The chapter ends in joy because God's salvation reaches beyond Israel to all nations watching his victory.
key_points: God is praised for faithfulness and perfect faithfulness.
The Lord becomes a refuge for the poor and needy.
Death is swallowed up and tears are wiped away.
The nations witness God's saving hand on Zion.

title: The strong city, perfect peace, and resurrection hope
summary_text: The redeemed sing of a strong city whose walls are salvation and whose gates are open to the righteous. Trust in the Lord brings perfect peace, and waiting on him is better than trusting human security. The chapter ends with a resurrection hope and a plea to hide until the indignation has passed.
key_points: God keeps in perfect peace the mind fixed on him.
The wicked do not learn righteousness even when shown grace.
The dead will live and awake when God visits.
The people are called to wait until wrath has passed.

title: The guarded vineyard and the trumpet of return
summary_text: The Lord defeats Leviathan and then turns to his vineyard, promising careful protection instead of wrath. Though discipline has scattered the people, he will gather them one by one, from the river to the valley of Egypt. A great trumpet will announce their return and the end of exile.
key_points: The Lord destroys the monster imagery of chaos.
His vineyard is guarded and watered with care.
Exiles will be gathered one by one from far places.
The trumpet signals restoration and worship.
evidence_phrases: I the Lord do keep it

title: Woe to Ephraim and the tested cornerstone
summary_text: The intoxicated leaders of Ephraim boast, but their crown is fading and judgment is coming. The Lord offers a firm foundation stone in Zion, yet the people prefer a covenant with death and lies. The chapter ends by showing that God's strange work of judgment is also his deliberate wisdom.
key_points: Pride and drunkenness make leaders unable to hear God.
The Lord lays a precious cornerstone in Zion.
Human bargains with death cannot survive judgment.
God's correction is precise, measured, and purposeful.
evidence_phrases: woe to the crown of pride

title: Ariel, the sealed book, and heartless worship
summary_text: Jerusalem is named Ariel and warned that siege and disappointment are coming, though the attackers will suddenly vanish by God's hand. The people honor God with lips while their hearts stay far away, and the wise are baffled because the word is sealed to the proud. Yet the humble will rejoice when the deaf hear and the blind see.
key_points: Judgment presses the city but does not defeat God.
Lip service cannot substitute for heart obedience.
The wise and learned are confounded by sealed truth.
God will yet give sight and understanding to the meek.
evidence_phrases: the vision of all has become

title: Woe to stubborn children and the call to quiet trust
summary_text: Judah is condemned for seeking Egypt's help instead of listening to the Lord, and the alliance will only deepen shame. Yet the Lord still offers mercy: repentance, quietness, and trust would bring strength, while rebellion brings distress. Even so, God promises to defeat Assyria and bless the people with healing and instruction.
key_points: Egypt cannot rescue a people who refuse God's word.
Returning and rest are the path to strength.
The Lord will be gracious after judgment.
Assyria will be struck down by the voice of God.

title: Woe to those who trust Egypt
summary_text: The chapter repeats the warning that political horses and chariots cannot save Judah from the coming judgment. The Lord is not against defense itself but against misplaced confidence that forgets the Holy One of Israel. As a bird hovers over its nest, so the Lord will defend Zion and cause Assyria to fall.
key_points: Trusting in horses is still trusting in flesh.
God alone can defend Jerusalem like a hovering bird.
When the Lord rises, human helpers collapse.
The enemy will be driven back by divine power.
evidence_phrases: woe to those who go down to Egypt

title: A king in righteousness and the outpouring of the Spirit
summary_text: A righteous king will rule with justice, and his reign will expose the difference between the foolish and the noble. When the Spirit is poured out, the wilderness becomes fruitful and security replaces complacency. The chapter looks forward to a transformed society where righteousness produces peace and lasting confidence.
key_points: A true king rules in justice and shelters the weak.
The Spirit changes dry places into fruitful land.
Foolishness and complacency will no longer be hidden.
Peace grows where righteousness is honored.
evidence_phrases: a king shall reign in righteousness
the Spirit is poured upon us
the wilderness becomes a fruitful field
my people shall dwell in peace

title: Woe to the spoiler and Zion's secure future
summary_text: A destroyer who has not yet been repaid is warned that his own ruin will come, while God's people pray for mercy and stability. The Lord will be exalted as judge, lawgiver, and king, and Zion will be a place where no one says, 'I am sick.' The city's future depends not on human strength but on the presence of its saving God.
key_points: The spoiler will eventually be spoiled.
The righteous seek mercy and stability from God.
The Lord fills Zion with justice and wisdom.
The city is secure because God dwells there.

title: Judgment on the nations and Edom's desolation
summary_text: The prophet summons the nations to hear a universal day of slaughter in which the Lord's sword is filled with blood. Edom stands as the picture of total ruin, with land, sky, and people all under judgment. The chapter is severe because it shows that rebellion against God's justice ends in complete desolation.
key_points: The Lord's anger reaches beyond one nation to the world.
Edom becomes a symbol of irreversible ruin.
No earthly power can resist the divine decree.
God's justice is exact and comprehensive.
evidence_phrases: for my sword is bathed in heaven
the land shall become burning pitch

title: The wilderness blossoms and the ransomed return
summary_text: After judgment, the desert itself bursts into flower and the weak are strengthened by God's saving power. The chapter encourages the fearful with the promise of healing, holiness, and a highway for the redeemed. What had been barren becomes the pathway home for the Lord's rescued people.
key_points: The wilderness will rejoice and blossom abundantly.
The Lord strengthens the weak hands and feeble knees.
The way of holiness belongs to the redeemed.
Sorrow and sighing flee as the ransomed return.
evidence_phrases: the wilderness and the dry land

title: Rabshakeh's taunt against Jerusalem
summary_text: Assyria sends Rabshakeh to intimidate Jerusalem and break Hezekiah's trust in the Lord. He mocks Judah's defenses, questions their loyalty to Egypt, and claims that no god has rescued the nations already conquered by Assyria. The chapter is a test of faith: will the people answer with fear or with confidence in the Lord's word?
key_points: Assyria tries to frighten the people with propaganda.
Egypt cannot rescue Judah from the assault.
Rabshakeh despises the Lord's power and mockingly compares gods.
Silence and trust are wiser than answering the blasphemy.
evidence_phrases: do not let Hezekiah deceive you
has any of the gods delivered

title: Hezekiah prays and the Lord saves Jerusalem
summary_text: Hezekiah turns the blaspheming letter into prayer, asking the Lord to defend his own name against Assyria. Isaiah sends the word that the enemy's arrogance has reached God's ears and that Jerusalem will survive. The angel of the Lord then strikes down the Assyrian camp, and Sennacherib ends in shame.
key_points: Hezekiah spreads the threatening letter before the Lord.
Isaiah announces that God has heard the blasphemy.
Jerusalem will survive because of God's zeal.
The Assyrian army falls by divine intervention.

title: Hezekiah's sickness, sign, and song
summary_text: Hezekiah receives word that he will die, then pleads for healing and is granted fifteen more years. The shadow moving backward becomes a sign that the Lord can reverse what seems final. The chapter closes with Hezekiah's song, which turns personal rescue into public thanksgiving.
key_points: The king is told to set his house in order.
The Lord hears prayer and restores life.
The sun's shadow moving back serves as a sign.
Hezekiah answers mercy with a written song of praise.

title: Babylonian envoys and the coming exile
summary_text: Hezekiah proudly shows Babylon his treasures, and Isaiah uses the visit to announce that those riches will one day be carried away. What looks like a diplomatic honor becomes a prophetic warning about future exile. The chapter closes with a sober reminder that the kingdom will not stand forever in the hands of its present rulers.
key_points: Receiving envoys can become an occasion for self-display.
Isaiah interprets the visit as a sign of coming loss.
The royal treasury will not remain untouched.
Peace in one generation may hide trouble for the next.
evidence_phrases: all the house of his precious things

title: Comfort for Zion and the greatness of God
summary_text: The second major movement of Isaiah opens with comfort and a call to prepare the way for the Lord. God is presented as the shepherd who measures the waters, lifts up the weak, and rules above the nations without rival. The chapter replaces despair with awe, insisting that the Creator is both near to the weary and beyond comparison.
key_points: Comfort is announced because the punishment is ending.
The Lord's way must be prepared in the wilderness.
God measures all nations like dust.
Those who wait on him will renew their strength.
evidence_phrases: prepare the way of the Lord

title: Fear not, for I am with you
summary_text: The Lord challenges the nations and idols to prove themselves, then reassures his servant not to fear because he is holding him. The chapter mixes courtroom language with pastoral comfort, showing that the same God who judges false gods also strengthens the helpless. Israel is told that the Holy One will help and uphold them by his righteous hand.
key_points: The nations and idols cannot answer God's questions.
Fear is answered by God's personal presence.
The Lord upholds the servant with righteous power.
The poor and needy will rejoice in the Holy One of Israel.

title: The servant of the Lord and the blind people
summary_text: The servant comes quietly with justice for the nations, not by crushing weakness but by faithfully bringing light. Yet Israel remains blind and deaf despite being God's covenant people, so the Lord exposes their inability to see what is in front of them. The chapter ends by calling the people to listen before darkness hardens into loss.
key_points: The servant brings justice without crushing the bruised.
God opens blind eyes and sets captives free.
Israel itself is blind to the covenant it was given.
The proper response is to hear and obey before further judgment.

title: Redeemed by name and called as witnesses
summary_text: God reminds Israel that he created, formed, and redeemed them by name, so they need not fear waters, fire, or exile. He alone saves, and he summons his people to be witnesses that no other god can compare with him. The chapter combines comfort and testimony, showing that redemption leads to public confession of God's uniqueness.
key_points: God claims Israel as his own through creation and redemption.
Water and fire cannot defeat the people God keeps.
The Lord alone is Savior and Redeemer.
His people are called to testify to his identity.
evidence_phrases: I have called you by your name
when you pass through the waters
I even I am the Lord
do not remember the former things

title: Israel chosen, idols exposed, Cyrus named
summary_text: The Lord pours out his Spirit on Israel, promising blessing and renewal while mocking the absurdity of idol-making. Human hands carve out what cannot speak, move, or save, but the living God foretells the future and names Cyrus as his shepherd. The contrast between false gods and the Lord's sovereign word is the chapter's central point.
key_points: God will pour his Spirit and refresh his people.
Idols are nothing but carved material and deception.
The Lord declares future events before they happen.
Cyrus will be used to rebuild Jerusalem.
evidence_phrases: he will perform all my pleasure

title: Cyrus anointed and all nations summoned to turn
summary_text: The Lord calls Cyrus by name and empowers him so that the world may know that salvation comes from the God of Israel. He insists that there is no other God, no rival creator, and no hidden counsel that can thwart his purpose. Even the nations are invited to turn to him and be saved, because righteousness and strength are found nowhere else.
key_points: Cyrus is named before he ever rules.
No other god can compare with the Lord.
The ends of the earth are summoned to turn and be saved.
Every knee will bow before God's righteous rule.
evidence_phrases: a righteous God and a Savior

title: Idols carried by beasts, God carries his people
summary_text: Babylon's gods are shown as burdens that must be carried, bowed down, and rescued, while the Lord carries his people from birth to gray hair. The chapter is a sharp contrast between dead idols and the living God who declares the end from the beginning. His purpose cannot be moved by human effort or pagan ritual.
key_points: Idols are not saviors; they are loads to transport.
The Lord has carried his people from the womb onward.
God declares the end from the beginning.
His counsel will stand and he will do all his pleasure.

title: Babylon brought down from queen to slave
summary_text: Babylon, once proud and secure, is told to sit in the dust and accept humiliation. The chapter condemns its sorceries, arrogance, and confidence that no one sees its wickedness. The Lord's judgment will leave the city stripped of the power it once used to dominate others.
key_points: The proud city is sent from throne to dust.
Occult power and secret schemes cannot save it.
Babylon's claim of permanence is exposed as false.
The Lord's vengeance reaches the boastful empire.
evidence_phrases: I shall not sit as a widow
you have trusted in your wickedness
there is none to save you

title: Refined Israel and peace like a river
summary_text: God addresses stubborn Israel, reminding them that he foretold events beforehand so they would not claim idols or luck as the cause. He refines them for his name's sake and calls them out of Babylon so they can live in peace. The chapter ends with a simple invitation: hear, learn, and walk in the way of the Lord.
key_points: God's prophecy is meant to keep pride from taking credit.
Israel is refined, not abandoned, for God's own name.
Leaving Babylon is part of obeying the Lord.
Peace comes like a river to the obedient.

title: The servant called from the womb and light to the nations
summary_text: The servant speaks of being formed by God before birth and sent not only to gather Israel but also to bring light to the nations. Though Zion feels forgotten, the Lord answers with maternal tenderness and promises that kings and nations will be drawn into her restoration. The chapter expands the scope of salvation far beyond one people.
key_points: God prepares his servant before birth.
The servant's mission reaches Israel and the Gentiles.
Zion is not forgotten even when she feels abandoned.
Kings and nations will respond to God's restoring work.
evidence_phrases: kings shall be your nursing fathers

title: The servant's obedience amid suffering
summary_text: The servant declares that the Lord has not failed to hear, but he has opened the servant's ear for willing obedience. Though struck, spat upon, and opposed, the servant keeps trusting the Lord who helps and vindicates him. The chapter highlights obedient suffering as part of God's saving purpose.
key_points: God gives the servant an opened ear and a taught tongue.
Suffering does not mean the Lord has abandoned him.
The servant sets his face like flint toward obedience.
Those who trust in their own fire will lie down in sorrow.
evidence_phrases: the Lord has opened my ear
I gave my back to those who strike
I set my face like flint
who among you fears the Lord
walk in the light of your fire

title: Awake, Zion, and drink no more the cup of wrath
summary_text: The Lord tells the faithful to look back to Abraham and forward to a renewed Zion, because his righteousness and salvation are near. The cup of wrath that once pressed the city will be removed and given to her oppressors instead. The chapter comforts the broken with a promise that God's redeeming power is stronger than their shame.
key_points: God's saving righteousness will not fail.
Zion is told to awake from grief and fear.
The cup of wrath will pass from the faithful to the enemies.
The Lord's salvation is near enough to be trusted.
evidence_phrases: I have taken out of your hand

title: Zion awakened and the good news of peace
summary_text: Zion is called to wake up, put on strength, and shake off the dust because the Lord is redeeming her. The chapter celebrates the messenger who brings good news, the beauty of peace, and the freedom of the people who see God's salvation. What was once defiled will be holy again because the Lord has bared his holy arm before all the nations.
key_points: The city must wake up because God's rescue is near.
The messenger of peace announces the Lord's reign.
The people depart in purity and gladness.
God reveals his holy arm to the nations.

title: The suffering servant bears sin
summary_text: The servant is despised, rejected, and acquainted with grief, yet his suffering is not wasted because it bears the sins of many. He is wounded for transgressions, crushed for iniquities, and still silent before his oppressors. The chapter stands at the heart of Isaiah by explaining how God's servant can bring healing through substitution.
key_points: The servant suffers innocently for others' sins.
His wounds bring healing and peace.
He is led like a lamb to slaughter without complaint.
The Lord makes his soul an offering for sin.
evidence_phrases: like a lamb to the slaughter
his soul an offering for sin
he bore the sin of many

title: The barren woman becomes fruitful again
summary_text: Zion, once barren and ashamed, is told to sing because her children will multiply beyond expectation. The Lord promises a covenant of peace, enduring compassion, and protection from every weapon forged against her. The chapter turns from exile language to permanent security under God's faithful love.
key_points: Barren Zion will be suddenly fruitful.
The Lord's covenant of peace will not be removed.
No weapon formed against God's people will prosper.
Former shame gives way to lasting security.

title: Come to the waters and seek the Lord
summary_text: The chapter invites the thirsty to come without money and receive what cannot be bought. God's word is promised to accomplish his purpose, just as rain and snow do not return empty. The closing call is to seek the Lord while he may be found and to leave behind the old life of sin.
key_points: True life is offered freely to the thirsty.
God's word will succeed in its mission.
Seeking the Lord requires leaving sin behind.
Joy and peace follow those who hear and turn.

title: Salvation for foreigners and judgment on blind watchmen
summary_text: Isaiah announces that foreigners and eunuchs who keep God's covenant will not be excluded from his house. But Israel's leaders are condemned as blind watchmen and greedy shepherds who care for themselves instead of the flock. The chapter joins open welcome with severe warning, showing that God's house must be holy and just.
key_points: The Lord welcomes outsiders who join themselves to him.
His house is a house of prayer for all peoples.
Blind watchmen and greedy shepherds are condemned.
Privilege without faithfulness invites judgment.

title: The lofty and the lowly, and peace to the contrite
summary_text: The righteous perish while idolatry and spiritual adultery continue in the land, exposing a people who trust in ritual without repentance. Yet the Lord also declares that he dwells with the humble and contrite, reviving the lowly and healing the broken. Peace belongs to those who turn from idols and listen to his voice.
key_points: The death of the righteous is not meaningless to God.
Idolatry continues to draw the nation away from truth.
God dwells with the humble and lowly in spirit.
Peace is promised to the near and the far.
evidence_phrases: I dwell in the high and holy place

title: True fasting, justice, and Sabbath delight
summary_text: The Lord rejects fasting that is only ritual while injustice and quarrels continue unchanged. He describes the fast he chooses as one that loosens oppression, feeds the hungry, and cares for the needy. When Sabbath delight replaces selfish labor, the people discover light, healing, and restored joy.
key_points: Empty religious performance does not satisfy God.
Real fasting breaks chains and shares bread.
Mercy and justice are inseparable from worship.
Delighting in the Sabbath leads to joy in the Lord.
evidence_phrases: the fast that I have chosen
deal your bread to the hungry

title: Sin separates, but the Redeemer comes
summary_text: The problem is not that God is powerless, but that sin has separated the people from him and filled the land with violence and lies. When no human helper appears, the Lord himself puts on righteousness like armor and comes as Redeemer. The chapter ends with a covenant promise that his Spirit and word will remain with the people forever.
key_points: Sin blocks fellowship and muffles prayer.
The land is filled with injustice and bloodshed.
God himself comes to rescue when no one else can.
His covenant and Spirit endure for his people.
evidence_phrases: my Spirit that is upon you

title: Zion's light and the nations' glory
summary_text: Zion is told to arise because the glory of the Lord has come upon her and the nations will be drawn to that light. Wealth, sons, daughters, and kings come in procession, showing that God's salvation reshapes the world's center. What was once small and despised becomes a city of everlasting splendor.
key_points: God's light turns Zion into a beacon.
The nations and their treasures stream toward her.
The Lord becomes an everlasting light.
Smallness is replaced by beauty, peace, and honor.
evidence_phrases: nations shall come to your light
a little one shall become a thousand

title: The anointed servant and the year of favor
summary_text: The servant speaks with the Spirit's anointing to bring good news to the poor, liberty to captives, and comfort to those who mourn. The chapter announces the year of the Lord's favor and the rebuilding of ruined places through joy rather than shame. Zion is clothed as a bride because salvation and righteousness now define her identity.
key_points: The Spirit-anointed servant proclaims liberation.
The year of favor replaces the day of vengeance.
Ruined places are rebuilt and renamed with honor.
God clothes his people with salvation and praise.
evidence_phrases: the year of the Lord's favor

title: Zion's vindication and the watchmen who remind God
summary_text: Zion's righteousness will shine so brightly that the nations can no longer ignore her restored identity. Watchmen are set on the walls to cry out until the Lord establishes Jerusalem as a praise in the earth. The chapter paints redemption as both public vindication and vigilant prayer.
key_points: Zion will no longer be forsaken or called desolate.
The Lord appoints watchmen who will not keep silent.
A new name marks restored belonging.
God's people are prepared as a holy people and a joyful bride.

title: The divine warrior and remembered mercy
summary_text: A warrior comes from Edom with garments stained by judgment, announcing that he has trodden the winepress alone. The scene then shifts into a prayer that remembers God's past mercy, confesses present trouble, and asks him to return for the sake of his people. The chapter joins awe at God's vengeance with longing for his kindness.
key_points: God alone executes the winepress of judgment.
His past kindness to Israel is recalled in prayer.
The people confess that they need his fatherly pity.
Mercy and holiness are both needed in the final plea.
evidence_phrases: I have trodden the winepress alone

title: Oh that you would rend the heavens
summary_text: The prayer asks God to break through the heavens as he once did in power and to make his name known among the nations. In confessing sin, the people admit that all their righteousness is like filthy cloth and that they are clay in the hands of the potter. The chapter is a plea for mercy from a people who know they cannot save themselves.
key_points: The people ask God to come down in visible power.
Sin has made their righteousness unclean.
They confess that he is the potter and they are the clay.
Mercy must come from God because no one else can help.

title: New heavens and a new earth for the faithful
summary_text: The Lord distinguishes between those who stubbornly provoke him and those who answer when he calls. Judgment falls on the rebels, but his servants inherit new heavens, a new earth, and a joy that does not fade. The chapter ends with a creation renewed enough that grief, danger, and death are no longer the final word.
key_points: God is found by those who do not continue in rebellion.
The rebellious receive measured judgment.
Servants inherit blessing and delight.
New creation replaces sorrow and old ruin.
evidence_phrases: I was found by those who did not seek me
new heavens and a new earth

title: Heaven is my throne and all nations come to worship
summary_text: The book closes by rejecting empty religion and honoring the humble, contrite person who trembles at God's word. Jerusalem is pictured as a mother giving birth in sudden joy, and the nations are gathered as witnesses to God's final work. The last vision is sweeping and sober: God's servants endure, God's enemies are judged, and all flesh comes to worship before him.
key_points: God values the humble and rejects self-made religion.
Jerusalem will experience both sudden birth and renewed joy.
The Lord judges all nations and all flesh.
The chapter ends with worship extending to every people.
evidence_phrases: this is the one I will look to
all flesh shall come to worship
